# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for a React application. Install Node.js, run npm install, build the production bundle with npm run build, and serve it with a lightweight HTTP server. Keep the Dockerfile simple and easy to understand for our team.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official node runtime as base image  // Image from docker hub Node is based on Alpine Linux , therefore faster build uptimes, less layer count etc..
FROM node:latest # You can use the specific tag if you have it. e.g., FROM node:14-alpine3.8 (use latest by default).  The version of 'node' at time was v7 and then updated to align with current LTS release which is Node.js Latest Version
# Set environment variables for the running app
ENV NODE_ENV=production # setting up a new variable in production, so that if there are any dependencies it will use those specified here  (instead of being included at runtime)   // If you're using create-react-app this is not necessary. However we have to set environment variables or they won’t get picked by default
# Copy over your app into the container for processing, /src directory from current working dir/ => Your App source code  (i think it has something like C:/Users/\.\./your_project)   // if you're using create-react-app this is not necessary. However we have to copy our application or they won’t be available at runtime
# Install your app dependencies in the working directory as per NPM INSTALL --save (make sure these packages are already defined within package_lock file and node modules)   // You can install from .env using npm i.e., we will need to make it generic eith yarn or create-react-app's default settings
# Install the 'serve', a http server for Node apps in docker  (if you haven’t done so already). The --force flag ensures that new packages are installed and their dependencies upgraded, thus avoiding old outdated versions being used which could cause errors.
RUN npm install -g serve # This command should be run inside your Dockerfile after copying the required files from host machine to docker image   // if you're using create-react-app this is not necessary but it will save our work later, since we are now doing a lot of manual operations on container
# Install npm packages into current working directory in production mode. (This should be done after copying the app files)  [--force] flag ensures that new dependencies get installed and their corresponding versions upgraded to avoid errors from older outdated version(s). If you forget this step, your docker image won't run as expected due npm packages not getting up-graded or could still use old ones.
RUN npm install --only=production # This command should be after copying the required files and is used to ensure that production dependencies are installed in a Docker environment which would get rid of any outdated versions (if they exist) if we're using yarn, create-react-app etc  // We don’t need npm i again since packages were already defined inside package.json
# Run your app with NPM RUN -- This command is to make sure that the scripts within our 'package.jsons' run properly in an interactive mode (Node Inspector or browser). If there are no errors, then this step allows you for debugging while developing on Windows machine from inside Docker container
RUN npm run build # Run when copying necessary files into docker image with --no-save option to prevent cache issues during builds. This command will execute the prebuild script in your package’s “scripts” section (which are usually `npm start` and others like it). Also, copy production bundle over
# Expose port so that we can access our server outside of Docker: // HTTP/HTTPS default ports; Node is using environment variable for these details. For both http & https use same PORT but have different domain names if you need to make your app publicly accessible with multiple domains e,g., localhost and docker-hostname
EXPOSE 80 # Http Port which our application uses by the serve server (default) - we can change it as per requirement. However this port should not be exposed or changed unless absolutely necessary due security considerations // If you're using create-react-app, don’t worry about exposing http/https ports otherwise they will get defaulted to random openable Ports and may cause inadvertent access
EXPOSE 4000 # Https Port which our application uses by the serve server (default) - we can change it as per requirement. However this port should not be exposed or changed unless absolutely necessary due security considerations // If you're using create-react-app, don’t worry about exposing http/https ports otherwise they will get defaulted to random openable Ports and may cause inadvertent access
# CMD instruction [run app on container startup]. This command provides the way for Docker daemon how it should run your application. In a normal development setup, you'd want this set as simple just 'npm start', but if there are dependencies or other issues at runtime then specify those details here // If not using create-react-app ensure that we have CMD [ "node", "./bin/www" ]
CMD ["serve"] # Default command to run when starting up the docker image, in this case it will start http server for production app and serve static files from our built React application. This is a one liner if there's no other commands specified under CMD as well (similar with --no-save)
